summary: Automatically symbolized function-pointer data across the assembly by replacing raw .4byte address literals in asm/data.s with func_XXXXXXXX labels and by adding missing .global func_XXXXXXXX labels at code entry points across text and fighter assembly files. The PR body states this covered pointers to functions from 0x80000000 through 0x803B723C and was generated by a Python script.
what_changed: In asm/data.s, many function-pointer table entries changed from raw address literals such as .4byte 0x800E1040 to symbolic references such as .4byte func_800E1040.
NULL entries and apparent non-function constants were left as-is in the shown data tables.
The diff added many .global func_XXXXXXXX declarations and matching labels immediately before existing assembly instructions in asm/text_3.s, asm/text_5.s, and numerous fighter assembly files.
The largest data-side edit was asm/data.s with 2804 added and 2804 deleted lines, indicating mostly one-for-one pointer spelling changes.
The largest label additions were asm/text_5.s with 2808 added lines and asm/text_3.s with 1887 added lines.
Fighter assembly files received smaller batches of labels, including Kirby, Crazy Hand, Master Hand, many playable fighters, Sandbag, and Zako variants.
smart_moves: Used symbolic function labels rather than leaving raw absolute addresses in function-pointer tables, making later decompilation, renaming, and xref searching easier.
Added labels at the actual code addresses targeted by data pointers, so .4byte func_XXXXXXXX references had concrete assembler symbols to resolve.
Kept the mechanical transformation narrow: pointer spelling and label declarations changed, while existing instruction bodies were not rewritten in the excerpt.
Preserved repeated function pointers as repeated symbolic references, e.g. duplicate entries became duplicate func_8011F26C references rather than being collapsed or reinterpreted.
Automation was appropriate for the scale: the PR body explicitly says the changes were made automatically using a Python script.
decomp_lessons: Before converting large data tables into C structs, first replace raw function-address constants with labels; this exposes table shape and callback roles without changing behavior.
Function-pointer tables are easier to audit when entries point at func_XXXXXXXX symbols, especially tables annotated with roles such as Anim, Input, Action, Collision, and Camera.
Bulk symbolization can be a safe preparatory step if it is one-for-one, preserves NULL holes, and only adds labels at existing instruction boundaries.
Adding labels in assembly can improve future matching work by creating stable names that can later be replaced with semantic names as functions are understood.
When using scripts for broad asm cleanup, keep the transformation simple and evidence-preserving so reviewers can reason about it as address-to-symbol conversion rather than functional logic.
naming_conventions: Unnamed functions were labeled with the pattern func_XXXXXXXX, where XXXXXXXX is the original absolute address in uppercase hexadecimal, e.g. func_800E1040 and func_8027CF00.
Existing data labels such as lbl_803C15F8 and lbl_803C167C were retained.
NULL pointer entries remained spelled NULL rather than being replaced with numeric zero or a symbol.
The PR did not introduce semantic names for the functions in the excerpt; it standardized address-derived placeholder names.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace .4byte 0x8XXXXXXX entries that are known function addresses with .4byte func_8XXXXXXX.
For every referenced address that lacked a named entry point, insert .global func_XXXXXXXX followed by func_XXXXXXXX: immediately before the instruction at that address.
Use address-derived names to avoid guessing function purpose while still making linker-visible symbols.
Leave data-table alignment, table labels, comments, NULLs, and surrounding layout intact.
This tactic is especially useful for callback tables and action-state tables before moving them from asm/data.s into typed C data.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the provided PR slice.
No explicit reviewer rationale or requested changes are available; conclusions are based on the PR title, body, file list, and diff excerpt.
